preference:
“I prefer explanations, as a clinician, that provide a shortcut to the final inhibitor, allowing me to click to expand more details if I choose.”
“I prefer explanations that present a straight-line summary from gene → inhibitor → outcome, with a ‘+’ on nodes to reveal deeper context.”
“I prefer explanations that include phenotypes, transcriptomic data, and clinical metrics (e.g., IMDC score) alongside each gene to guide treatment decisions.”

“I prefer explanations that avoid ambiguous regulation terms like ‘regulation of programmed…’ by clearly specifying whether a gene up-regulates or down-regulates a process and stating the mutant’s consistent effect.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid mid-path sibling relations like ‘subclass of’ and instead use direct gene→process links.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid zig-zagging on the same relation (e.g., A ← inhibited by ← B → inhibits → C) to preserve causal clarity.”
“I prefer explanations that include an explicit check confirming each intermediate gene genuinely connects to the MTOR inhibitor, so no step feels arbitrary.”
“I prefer explanations that include the mutation phenotype (e.g., consistent up- or down-regulation) so clinicians can judge whether an inhibitor or activator is appropriate.”
“I prefer explanations that rely on direct, validated knowledge-graph relations rather than indirect subclass inferences.”
“I prefer explanations that are delivered as concise bullet points and clear, patient-level summaries.”

“I prefer explanations delivered as concise bullet points with an embedded graph—clinicians can scan key points while researchers expand layers.”
“I prefer explanations that link additional inhibitors (e.g., MTOR inhibitor) even when they aren’t on the direct path, to provide useful context.”
“I prefer explanations that include patient-level statements like ‘This patient fits trial population X based on features A, B, C.’”

“I prefer explanations for clinicians that focus only on biomarkers and outcomes rather than full mechanistic processes.”
“I prefer explanations that hide intermediate biological details when they don’t affect the clinical decision.”

“I prefer explanations that block nodes to check their importance and select dominator nodes that appear most frequently.”
“I prefer explanations that substitute the drug node with another from the same family to see if the explanations share common nodes.”
“I prefer explanations that use repetition of the same meta-pattern as a robustness check—balanced against diversity of information.”
“I prefer explanations that add counterfactual options like ‘see more similar drugs’ and make the graph dynamic so users can click to change features.”
“I prefer explanations that present a connected graph with just a few key nodes, keep colors, and offer both diagram and text views.”

“I prefer explanations that present an abstraction with minimal detail—showing only the most relevant nodes to keep it digestible.”
“I prefer explanations that highlight the single most important node in each path.”
“I prefer explanations that explicitly correlate each gene to its inhibitor.”
“I prefer explanations that vary in length—concise for clinicians and detailed for researchers.”

“I prefer explanations that evaluate whether the tumor is ‘hot’ or ‘cold’ to inform immune-cell interactions.”
“I prefer explanations that track tumor progression and drug resistance over time to suggest alternative treatments or combinations.”
“I prefer explanations that always show combined treatment options, potentially with AI-driven combination recommendations.”

“I prefer explanations that highlight clear, informative links—such as ‘dependence on opiates’—when they definitively identify the drug’s class.”
“I prefer explanations that include uniquely informative connections—like ‘dependence on opiates’—over vague or redundant links.”

“I prefer explanations that provide clearer mechanistic context, recognizing that most users lack a life-sciences background.”
“I prefer explanations that indicate whether each gene is up-regulated or down-regulated.”
“I prefer explanations that show multiple distinct gene paths converging on the same drug so users can identify common points.”

“I prefer explanations that indicate how many gene mutations are active in a patient, not just the top one.”
“I prefer explanations that are as short and concise as possible.”
“I prefer explanations that include the chemical formulas of the proteins generated to compare inhibitor/inhibited similarities.”
“I prefer explanations that merge rank and value to accumulate per-node scores, reducing overall size.”

“I prefer explanations that offer shorter, patient-friendly path summaries to improve understanding.”
“I prefer explanations that convert complex paths into natural-language summaries to avoid overwhelming patients.”
“I prefer explanations that use an LLM to translate and define each node, boosting clarity.”

“I prefer explanations that avoid placing ‘up’/‘down’ annotations in the middle of paths and instead clearly annotate regulation direction where it matters.”
“I prefer explanations that hide intermediate biology by default—revealing it on demand—so clinicians see only the gene→drug connection strength up front.”
“I prefer explanations that use ‘inhibits’ or ‘regulates’ links rather than generic predicates like ‘subclass of’ or ‘has participant in.’”
“I prefer explanations that include a combined score of all gene–drug connections, since both patients and clinicians need that aggregate metric for therapy predictions.”

“I prefer explanations that are simplified and avoid unclear descriptions that obscure the biological context.”
“I prefer explanations that include cellular protein localization to contextualize isoforms.”
“I prefer explanations that combine textual and graph-based visualizations to serve clinicians and researchers without overwhelming patients.”
“I prefer explanations that ensure specific information is always clear and complete.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid excessive detail that prevents moving from specific to general classes in one direction.”
“I prefer explanations that use precise class relations rather than overly general ones.”
“I prefer explanations that only include super-to-subclass hierarchies when they maintain correct directional flow.”
“I prefer explanations that penalize paths assuming genes are similar and would be inhibited similarly.”
“I prefer explanations that include additional superclass context when it adds value, even if the path is longer.”

“I prefer explanations that avoid convoluted paths like Gene → Process → Process → Gene and instead choose simpler, more intuitive routes.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid vague relations such as ‘acts upstream or within’ and use precise, descriptive links.”
“I prefer explanations that do not rely on analogies to similar genes—which can mislead patients—and instead provide direct evidence of mechanism.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid technical GO class labels like ‘subclass of’ when addressing general audiences.”

“I prefer explanations that provide sufficient bio-background support alongside graphical visualizations for patient comprehension.”
“I prefer explanations that include an extra textual description of the graphical visualization.”
“I prefer explanations that use specific terminology to increase clarity over more generic phrasing.”
“I prefer explanations that clearly define each gene’s type and its relation between specific subtype and drug.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid vague predicates like ‘acts upstream of or within’ and instead use precise regulatory descriptions.”

“I prefer explanations that include molecular function annotations for greater informativeness.”
“I prefer explanations that identify the direct superclass of the query gene when paths become too specific.”
“I prefer explanations that leverage sequence information to obtain domain motif annotations and complement the rationale.”
“I prefer explanations that balance process detail with readability—using technical paths sparingly when they add clear value.”
“I prefer explanations that use the gene class hierarchy to link directly to the inhibitor for clearer context.”
“I prefer explanations that define specific gene/protein types—explaining exactly what ‘class 3’ represents.”

“I prefer explanations that avoid technical jargon like ‘inhibitor’ for non-technical users and use intuitive terminology.”
“I prefer explanations that are shorter and more straightforward to build trust, while offering full technical detail for accountability when needed.”
“I prefer explanations that include a verbal user guide to clarify logical flow and the roles of inhibitors and processes for non-technical users.”

“I prefer explanations that merge regulation processes into a single, clear reasoning path rather than splitting them across multiple variants.”
“I prefer explanations that are concise by default but allow users to expand for more detail if desired.”
“I prefer explanations that limit the number of genes listed in a path to only those indispensable for understanding.”
“I prefer explanations that avoid redundant ‘Signaled Processes’ sections by not listing the same process twice.”
“I prefer explanations that streamline ‘Signaled Drugs’ views by showing only essential drugs rather than a long sequence.”
“I prefer explanations that include a reliability score for each drug to inform confidence in the recommendation.”

“I prefer explanations that include an explicit AI-generated justification element with each summary.”
“I prefer explanations that are short and specific for patients, but long and detailed for clinicians.”
“I prefer explanations that assist clinicians with deeper insights beyond the basics.”
“I prefer explanations that include a clinical-endorsement metric—showing how many experts back this structure—and a reliability score or margin of error.”
“I prefer explanations that include a legal disclaimer so clinicians retain final responsibility.”

“I prefer explanations that are simple and not overly complex overall.”
“I prefer explanations that include a margin-of-error and reliability indicator for each path.”
“I prefer explanations that omit overly general information that doesn’t aid decision-making.”
“I prefer explanations that offer multiple tailored versions for different user roles.”
